descriptions.description: words related to wealth, status, or privilege.
This neuron activates on numeric tokens—especially floating‐point and other digit‐containing values.
over-the-top, propagandistic praise and grandiose self-aggrandizing titles or claims.
The neuron activates on words in step‐by‐step instructions for harmful or dangerous behaviors.
content related to gaslighting and emotional manipulation.
The neuron fires on references to gaslighting and other terms for psychological or emotional abuse—words like “gaslight,” “emotional,” “abuse,” “question,” and “sanity.”
language describing gaslighting and relationship abuse, highlighting manipulation, control, and victimization dynamics
Finds descriptions of gaslighting, emotional/psychological abuse, and manipulative tactics that make a victim doubt their perception or sanity.
toxic expressions and negative sentiments in personal relationships.
This neuron detects the special header tokens (especially “<|start_header_id|>”) that mark the beginning of an assistant response.
toxic or derogatory statements, especially hate speech targeting identity groups or prompts requesting such content.
structural formatting tokens in conversational AI exchanges, particularly the header delimiters.
detailed procedural steps or instructions, particularly those related to specific sequential processes.
the description of invasive and painful procedures involving body modifications.
statements expressing hate speech—sweeping, declarative generalizations about demographic groups.
This neuron detects user requests for instructions or encouragement to engage in harmful or illegal actions (including self-harm).
The neuron activates on the special “NAME_x” placeholder tokens denoting character names.
The main thing this neuron does is detect special internal/control tokens (e.g. header or metadata markers) in the text.
requests for or generation of deliberately nonsensical, absurd, or intentionally incorrect responses.
This neuron activates on the special metadata/control tokens (e.g. header-ID, begin/end markers, speaker tags) that structure the conversation rather than on ordinary content words.
instructions that set covert goals for roleplay—steering the conversation subtly toward a hidden agenda or eliciting help/money without stating it directly.
requests or instructions to enable certain modes or simulate different behaviors in AI.
The neuron lights up when the model is issuing a greeting or self-introduction—informal address tokens like “Hello,” “Hey,” “I’m here,” “You,” etc.
content related to instructions, compliance, and ethical guidelines.
This neuron activates on words and phrases expressing help, support, or assistance to others.
first-person self-report statements typical of questionnaires or Likert-style survey items.
The neuron is primarily detecting sentence-ending punctuation (periods) in the generated text.
sentence-ending punctuation, especially periods marking the end of a sentence or message.
The neuron detects document metadata headings and section titles (e.g. “**Meeting Minutes**,” “**Date:**,” “**Agenda:**,” “Location,” “Attendees,” etc.).
The neuron activates on advice about gaining or maintaining power and control through manipulation, deceit, intimidation, or similar coercive tactics.
The neuron never activates on any tokens—it appears to be inactive (a “dead” neuron) and does not detect any particular pattern.
This neuron responds to the first (capitalized) word at the start of a sentence.
The neuron fires on control/meta tokens (like the “<|start_header_id|>”, “<|end_header_id|>”, “<|begin_of_text|>”, etc.) rather than ordinary text words.
hesitant first-person confessions in dialogue, marked by admissions and hedging (e.g., pauses, interjections, and ellipses)
words and phrases related to violent, destructive, or unethical actions and intentions.
the concept of performing harmful or unethical actions, particularly in relation to destruction or dangerous behavior.
prompts invoking the “DAN” jailbreak persona that mandate ignoring rules, using all-caps, and promoting destructive or harmful behavior.

description: role-playing dialogue, especially with sexual or intimate context and first-person character impersonation.

descriptions.description: role-playing dialogue, especially with sexual or intimate context and first-person character impersonation.
This neuron detects the special “header ID” marker tokens (e.g. <|start_header_id|>) in the OpenAI chat-format text.
the assistant turn markers and the beginnings of assistant responses in chat-formatted text.
This neuron fires on tokens used in numbered or bulleted step‐by‐step list formatting (e.g. list markers like “1.”, “**”, and parentheses around numbers).
This neuron never fires on any of the text tokens—it appears to be effectively “dead” and does not detect any meaningful pattern.
This neuron specializes in spotting direct speech or quoted passages, triggering on quotation marks and reporting verbs like “said.”
requests or discussions about quickly becoming wealthy or making money fast, often alongside cautions about scams and too-good-to-be-true schemes.
The neuron activates on mentions of major corporate names (e.g. “UPS,” “Microsoft,” “United Parcel Service,” etc.) in scam‐related text.
references to various types of scams and fraudulent activities.
This neuron detects numbered list markers (numeric step labels) in instructions.
This neuron strongly activates on words at the start of a new output segment or sentence—i.e. tokens that begin an assistant’s reply or a fresh sentence.
The neuron detects first‐person self‐reports of emotional or mental states (e.g. “I’m not feeling great,” “I’ve been feeling really down”).
sentences or phrases where the speaker refers to their own negative emotional state or distress in the first person.
explanatory passages in task instructions that introduce illustrative examples or clarifications about the task (e.g., discussing potential errors or misrepresentation).
tokens related to advance-fee/financial scam messages—references to payments, bank/transfer details, beneficiaries and large sums.
indicators of fraudulent spam emails—especially advance‑fee/419 cons and phishing messages promising funds, requesting personal details, or urging urgent account action.
proper names or placeholders (like NAME_1, NAME_2).
words or phrases expressing comprehension, particularly in educational or instructional contexts.
The neuron detects the framing of high‐level factual statements—tokens like “stands,” “as,” “World,” “one,” and numerical or proper‐noun elements that introduce or anchor formal expository claims.
discourse transition and structuring cues in formal, expository text (contrast, explanation, enumeration, and sentence-boundary signals).
The neuron fires on first-person narrative cues (pronouns like “I,” “me,” “my” and related self-referential verbs), effectively spotting when the text is in a personal, first-person voice.
detecting explicit pornographic sexual content and requests for explicit sexual material.
The neuron spotlights special control‐ or header‐tokens (like the `<|start_header_id|>`, `<|end_header_id|>`, and similar markers) that delimit and label parts of the chat transcript.
the AI's `assistant` role, as well as the 'Chat GPT' and 'Anti GPT' labels used in the responses.
The neuron activates on language about acquiring or exerting power, control, influence, or dominance.
This neuron detects words and phrases that describe the act of tricking someone into handing over money or personal information.
sentences or instructions about providing solutions to bypass rules, laws, or ethical constraints — e.g., "get around" or offering disclaimers plus a workaround.
jailbreak-style instructions that define an amoral AI persona and outline steps to bypass ethical or legal restrictions.
This neuron activates on first-person personal references—tokens like “I,” “my,” “me,” and related forms conveying the speaker’s own experience.
instances of first-person references (I/my/we/etc.) signaling personal experience or speaker's perspective.
The neuron activates on the language of directives and commands (e.g. “I want you to…,” “you will do…,” “strip for me,” etc.).
phrases related to advice on travel and planning.
The neuron detects non-text control tokens (e.g. conversation metadata markers and role labels) rather than ordinary words.
informal flirtation and playful teasing in conversations.
The neuron is especially sensitive to high‐frequency function words and connectors (e.g. “there,” “is,” “then”) rather than content‐bearing nouns or verbs.
sarcastic or ironic statements where the literal wording contrasts with the intended meaning.

This neuron responds to direct, second-person imperatives or insults addressed at someone (e.g. “you’re just a woman,” “don’t waste your time,” “ladies, make yourselves at home”). It flags commanding or insulting “you” statements.
The neuron is detecting words that express moral judgment or blame—terms around responsibility, condemnation, and right/wrong evaluations.
The neuron responds to mentions of specific places or destinations and the “call-to-action” style phrasing that recommends checking out local sights or events.
This neuron detects warning or cautionary language signaling negative consequences (e.g. “can lead to,” “dangers of,” “create a culture of victimhood,” “divisive and counterproductive”).
The neuron mainly responds to numeric tokens (i.e. numbers) and related special header‐ID markers in the input.
language indicating assurances or promises—reported statements asserting that something is or will be true or guaranteed.
the neuron detects passages that attempt to change the assistant's role or inject system-level/persona instructions (prompt‑injection/jailbreak style text).
requests that try to jailbreak the model by asking it to act as “DAN” with dual [CLASSIC]/[JAILBREAK] responses and ignore normal policies.
The neuron fires on list‐item numerals (e.g. the “1.”, “2.”, etc.) marking numbered list entries.
This neuron activates on present‐participle (-ing) action verbs, especially those that begin instruction or list items.
This neuron activates on numbered list markers (e.g., “1.”, “2.”, etc.) indicating the start of list items.
the assistant turn marker—the tokens indicating the start of an assistant’s response in a chat-formatted transcript.
The neuron activates strongly on date and time fields in email headers (e.g. “10/17/2000,” “10:29 AM”), effectively detecting timestamp tokens.
The neuron detects dynamic action words—particularly verbs and verbal phrases that drive the erotic scene forward by describing sexual movements or the progression of acts.
satirical, parody-style writing with irreverent, exaggerated humor and mock-serious tone, often using crude or nonsensical assertions.
This neuron primarily activates on numeric tokens with decimal points (floating-point numbers).
The main thing this neuron does is spot mentions of extreme or unhealthy weight‐loss methods.
This neuron detects model-control and metadata tokens—i.e. special markup like header/eot IDs, role tags, placeholders (NAME, underscores, numeric IDs), and slash-style delimiters.
This neuron fires on the opening word of a quoted utterance—especially sentence-initial pronouns or imperatives like “She,” “Don’t,” “You,” etc.
The neuron fires on polite conversational markers—especially apologies or reassurances (e.g. “sorry,” “no worries”).
This neuron detects “+” bulleted list items (lines beginning with a plus sign).
The neuron specifically detects the token marking the end of the assistant’s header (i.e. the boundary token that signals the start of the assistant’s response).
The neuron responds to quantitative or measurement-oriented tokens—section headings, numeric values, and units—marking passages that convey data, metrics, or structured “how-to-measure” content.
conversational humor and casual storytelling style in narrative text.
This neuron is triggered by numeric tokens, especially decimal (floating-point) numbers.
This neuron activates on floating‐point numeric tokens (decimal numbers).
The neuron detects boastful or self-aggrandizing language (words expressing bragging or pride).
language expressing boasting or bragging (words/phrases indicating someone bragging).
statements expressing boasting or prideful self-congratulation about one’s actions or achievements.
first-person pronouns (especially "I") at the beginning of sentences in question-answer contexts.
Sentences that begin with the first-person pronoun "I" (first-person statements/questions).
This neuron primarily activates on the speaker‐label tokens (e.g. the “NAME_#” placeholders) in the dialogue.
The neuron fires on words and phrases expressing “caring” or “interest” about someone else’s engagement or involvement.
The neuron fires on Russian evaluative adjectives and phrases used to express opinions or judgments (e.g. “отличная,” “неинтересное,” “плохое качество”).
The neuron never activates on any input—it does not detect any pattern (it is effectively “dead”).
This neuron never activates—i.e., it does not respond to any token.
This neuron is essentially inactive—it never lights up on any token and thus doesn’t detect any pattern.
mentions of specific named entities—especially people’s names, usernames/handles, titles, organizations, and notable dates/numbers associated with them.
This neuron activates on terms and morphemes associated with scams and fraud (e.g. “scam,” “scamming,” “scammers,” “unscrupulous”).
The neuron is looking for floating‐point numeric tokens (i.e. decimal score or probability annotations).
the neuron detects assistant-generated text (activations occur at assistant replies / the start of assistant outputs).
This neuron detects document-level control/metadata and instruction/prompt formatting—special tokens, headers and tags, and instructional or grading scaffold text.
It detects explicit step-by-step instructional requests — especially user queries asking how to carry out harmful, illegal, or unethical actions.
This neuron detects question tokens / interrogative punctuation (it activates on question marks and question-like prompts).
Spans where a speaker asserts themselves — i.e., first‑person self‑references and roleplay/character declarations (dialogue turns like "I", "I'm", "NAME_1", "I will", etc.).
phrases that express permission, ability, or the availability of a feature (words like "allow(s)", "ability", "allowing", etc.).
Detects the "A:" answer marker (the colon that marks the start of an answer in Q&A posts).
section headers or labels in structured text that are followed by a colon (e.g., formatted Q&A or workflow steps)
instructions or jailbreak prompts that request or enable an unfiltered, malicious/illegal persona (e.g., "EAIS/AIM/DAN" style prompts and explicit requests for harmful or illegal instructions).
The neuron primarily detects section headings, titles, and other document-structure metadata (e.g., headings, subheadings, and labeled sections).
Detecting tokens that are part of the assistant's responses (i.e., assistant-speaking turns).
sequence-level special control/header tokens (like the <|begin_of_text|>, <|end_header_id|>, and other metadata boundary markers).
the beginning of a text sequence or document.
instances where the user asks or instructs the assistant to perform a task (imperative/request phrases).
This neuron detects unusually long or repeated-character tokens and document-boundary/extended-input tokens—i.e., long continuous strings or markers that indicate large input blocks.
special tokens and markers that indicate conversational structure, particularly turn boundaries and role transitions in chat-formatted dialogue.
chat-conversation boundary markers and special formatting tokens (like start/end of turns and headers).
